```{r Assignment 5E, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
names(coef_PCDA)<-colnames(reduced_values)


# Find most important metabolites
names(sort(abs(coef_PCDA),decreasing = T)[1:5])

#Compute prediction
pred<-factor(ifelse(as.matrix(reduced_values)%*%coef_PCDA<0,"Control",'Treatment'))

# Compute no. misclassifications
data.frame(condition,pred)[condition!=pred,] # 3 misclassification

table(Truth=condition,Prediction=pred)

```

#### How many misclassifications did you make?

We find that the most important 5 metabolites in descending order of
effect size are LPC.18.2.0.0.\_feature2, PC.30.0.\_feature2,
LPC.20.3.\_feature1and2, LPC.17.1.0.0.\_feature2 and PC.35.3.\_feature1.

The PCDA classifier model misclassifies 3 samples: we see in the
confusion 1 control gets classified as being in the treatment group and
2 treatment group samples get mistakenly classified as a control sample,
as can be seen in the confusion matrix. Counting the treatment group as
positive and the controls as negative, we then get a false positive rate
of 10% and a false negative rate of 20%.

#### End of text block.

#### 5F) Interpretation.

1.  Discuss the similarities and differences between the PCA and PCDA.
    [1]\
2.  Discuss the difference between using t-test to make a Volcano plot
    and using PCDA to calculate important metabolites [1].

#### Put any text for interpretation 5F here.

An important thing to note is that PCA is a step in the process of PCDA.
They are therefore intimately related, although the PCA on its own
serves a very different purpose: it finds direction vectors that explain
the maximal amount of variance in the sample space. PCDA, on the other
hand, uses PCA as a means of dimensionality reduction, to then perform
an LDA in the lower dimensional space of the first $n$ PCs. This then
yields a discriminant vector, that is a vector $d$ maximising the ratio
$\frac{d^{\intercal}Bd}{d^{\intercal}Wd}$, where $B$ and $W$ are the
between group and within group covariance matrices, respectively. This
can be interpreted as maximising the level of separation of the two
groups.

When performing a multitude of t-tests to make a Volcano plot, we are
testing the effects of each metabolite (or variable in general) on the
response separately. Contrarily, in PCDA and LDA the discriminant vector
is a linear combination of the metabolite concentrations detected, and
the weights of this combination indicate the effect of that metabolite.
This is an important difference for a number of reasons. Firstly, the
Volcano plot approach suffers from the multiple testing problem and
requires corrections (such as FDR control) accordingly, while PCDA only
requires one test. Secondly, it is possible that no single metabolite
has a very strong effect, while a combination of metabolite levels has
much stronger predictive power. In this case there will be few important
metabolites in the Volcano plot, while the discriminant vector can pick
up on this combined effect.

#### End of text block.

### Assignment 6: ASCA (Johan Westerhuis, 16-01-2026)

For ASCA we will use the HDANOVA and the mixlm packages. Obtain the
latest version from CRAN.

```{r}
#install.packages("mixlm")
#install.packages("HDANOVA") 

library(mixlm)
library(HDANOVA)
```

Load the metabolomics_Values and the metabolomics_Exp files again on
which the ASCA model will be applied. Use the same set of features as in
the Classification assignment.

#### 6A)

Apply preprocessing of your data. Consider transformation, centering
and/or scaling the data. Make plots of the data (before and after the
preprocessing steps) to defend your choices. [3]

#### Add code for figures here

```{r Assignment 6A, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}

library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(ggrepel)

## Loading the data
metabolomic_values<-read.csv("df_metabolomics_Values.csv",row.names = 1)
metabolomic_exp<-read.csv("df_metabolomics_Exp.csv",row.names = 1)

# Selecting lipids
reduced_values<-metabolomic_values|>
  dplyr::select(80:613)

raw_sd_mean<-data.frame(mean=apply(reduced_values,2,mean),
                        sd=apply(reduced_values,2,sd))

set.seed=12
sel<-sample(seq_len(ncol(reduced_values)),25)

sel_data<-reduced_values[,sel]


boxplot(sel_data,las=2,cex=0.6,names = abbreviate(colnames(sel_data)), main="boxplot of 25 random metabolites before processing")

ggplot(raw_sd_mean,aes(x=mean,y=sd))+geom_point()+labs(x="Mean",y="Standard deviation",title="Mean-variance relationship for the lipid metabolome")

# Attempt for square root transform, which did not yield desired results

# sqrt_met<-sqrt(reduced_values-min(reduced_values))
# 
# boxplot(sqrt_met[,sel])
# sqrt_sd_mean<-data.frame(mean=apply(sqrt_met,2,mean),
#                         sd=apply(sqrt_met,2,sd))
# ggplot(sqrt_sd_mean,aes(x=log(mean),y=log(sd)))+geom_point()
# 

processed_metabolomic_values<-as.data.frame(scale(reduced_values))


boxplot(processed_metabolomic_values[,sel],las=2, cex=0,6, names = abbreviate(colnames(sel_data),minlength = 5),main="boxplot of 25 random metabolites after standardisation")




```

#### Add arguments here

We begin by visualising the data with a boxplot of 25 randomly selected
metabolites, along with a mean-standard deviation plot for the entire
data set. The boxplots suggests that the data is distributed fairly
symmetrically, and we observe no clear relation between the mean and
standard deviation, suggesting that the data is homoscedastic. However,
the features do differ greatly in their spread and center. For this
reason we opted to limit the data preprocessing to centering and scaling
the data. We then observe that the data are centered at 0 and exhibit
similar interquartile distances in the postprocessing boxplot.

#### 6B) Data handling

Make the data from wide to long format that is necessary for the asca
function in the HDANOVA package. Make sure the factors Sex and Group are
defined as factors. (See ASCA exercise) [2]

```{r Assignment 6B, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
long_met_data=data.frame(sex=factor(metabolomic_exp$Rat.Sex,labels=c("F","M")), 
                         group=factor(metabolomic_exp$Groups),
                         metab=I(as.matrix(processed_metabolomic_values)))


```

#### 6C) ASCA model:

1)  Create an ASCA model using the only the main factors Sex and Group.
    Do not consider interaction terms yet. [1]
2)  Explain the meaning of Sum.Sq and Expl.var. from the ASCA table, and
    explain how they are calculated. [1]
3)  What is your interpretation of the ASCA model? [1]

```{r Assignment 6C, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
asca_mod<-asca(metab~sex+group,data=long_met_data)

summary(asca_mod)
```

#### Give explanation and interpretation here

2)  In the ASCA table ,"Sum.Sq." represents the sum of squares
    associated with each factor in the model and quantifies how much of
    the total variability in the metabolomics data is explained by that
    factor. It is calculated by decomposing the total sum of squares of
    the data into contributions from each experimental factor and the
    residuals.

    Explained variance(Expl.var.) is the percentage of the total sum of
    squares that is explained by each factor. It is obtained by dividing
    the sum of squares of a given factor by the total sum of squares and
    multiplying by 100. This value indicates the relative importance of
    each factor in explaining the overall variability in the dataset.

3)  The ASCA results show that Sex explains a substantial proportion of
    the variability in the metabolomics data (45.22%), indicating strong
    systematic differences in metabolite profiles between sexes. In
    contrast, the Group factor (Control vs Training at 8 weeks) explains
    only a small fraction of the total variance (12.80%), suggesting
    that the training intervention has a relatively minor effect on the
    metabolomic profiles at this time point. A large proportion of the
    variance remains in the residuals (41.98%), which reflects
    individual variability and sources of variation not captured by the
    experimental factors included in the model. Overall, the ASCA model
    indicates that sex-related differences dominate the structured
    variation in the data, while the effect of training is comparatively
    weak.

#### End of interpretation

#### 6D)

1.  Show score and loading plots of PC1 vs PC2 of the Group factor.
    [0.5]
2.  Show the loading plots of PC1 vs PC2 of the Group factor. Only show
    the 20 most important variables where
    $Importance_j =\sqrt(p_{1j}^2 + p_{2j}^2)$. [1.5]
3.  Give your interpretation of the score and loading plots. [1]

```{r Assignment 6D, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}


scoreplot(asca_mod,factor="group",main="Score plot of the group factor",legend="bottomleft",projections = T)


loadingplot(asca_mod,factor="group",scatter=T,main="Loading plot of the group factor")              

load<-loadings(asca_mod,factor = "group")

Importance=sqrt(load[,1]^2+load[,2]^2)

idx<-which(Importance%in% sort(Importance,decreasing = T)[1:20])

load<-as.data.frame(load[idx,])
load$names<-rownames(load)


ggplot(load,aes(`Comp 1`, `Comp 2`,label=abbreviate(names)))+geom_point(size=0.5)+geom_text_repel(size=3,max.overlaps = 12)+labs(x='PC1 (64%)', y='PC2 (20%)',title="Loading plot of the 20 most important metabolites")


```

#### Give interpretation of 6D3 here

The score plot for the Group factor shows that most of the group-related
variation is captured by PC1 (64%), indicating that the main differences
between groups are driven by a single dominant component. Along this
axis, there is a clear positive shift from the control group towards the
1-2 weeks training groups. Interestingly, later training time points are
barely shifted relative to the controls, with the 8 weeks training group
even showing a negative shift. This suggests a metabolomic effect that
is time related, and on which the impact of training is unclear.

PC2 (20%) explains a smaller proportion of the variance and mainly
reflects within-group variability, as there is considerable overlap
between groups along this axis. We do observe that at 8 weeks time, the
control group and training group are fairly separated along this axis,
while intermediate time points in the training cohort overlap along the
PC2 axis and are therefore not separated.

The loading plot shows that the group-related separation is driven by a
limited number of metabolites, while most variables contribute only
weakly. The plot of the 20 most important metabolites confirms that
these compounds have the largest influence on the separation observed in
the score plot, particularly along PC1. Metabolites with similar loading
directions are positively associated, whereas metabolites loading in
opposite directions contribute to differences between early and late
training stages/conditions.

Overall, the score and loading plots indicate that the group effect is
present but moderate, with training inducing consistent but relatively
subtle changes in the metabolomic profile. We again observe that sex is
the dominant source of variance. This interpretation is in agreement
with the ASCA results, where the group factor explains a smaller
fraction of the total variance compared to other sources of variation.

#### 6E) Add the two-factor interaction Sex:Group to your model.

1.  Make a new model in which the two-factor interaction Sex:Group is
    also included in the model.[1]
2.  What is your conclusion of this ASCA model with interaction? [1]

```{r Assignment 6E ASCA permutation analysis, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
int_mod<-asca(metab~sex*group,data=long_met_data)

summary(int_mod)
```

#### Give your conclusion of the interaction model

Including the Sex:Group interaction in the ASCA model shows that sex
remains the dominant factor influencing the metabolomic profiles,
explaining the largest proportion of the total variance. The training
effect (Group) alone explains a much smaller fraction of the
variability, indicating that the overall impact of training at 8 weeks
is modest. The interaction term Sex:Group explains 7.72% of variance,
suggesting that the metabolic response to training differs slightly
between males and females. Although this interaction effect is smaller
than the main effects, it suggests the presence of sex-dependent
training responses rather than a uniform effect across sexes. A
substantial proportion of the variance remains in the residuals
(34.26%), reflecting biological variability and other sources of
variation not captured by the experimental factors included in the
model. Overall, the ASCA model with interaction indicates that while sex
is the dominant driver of metabolic variation, training induces modest
effects that partially depend on sex.

#### End of conclusion

#### 6F: Statistical Inference

1.  To conclude whether the main factors and interaction are
    significant, apply a permutation test and plot the permutation
    result using the permutation plot function for both main factors and
    the interaction.[1]
2.  Explain why in ASCA a permutation test is used and not an F-test.[1]
3.  What is your interpretation of the permutation test? [1]

```{r Assignment 6F ASCA permutation analysis, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
mod.perm<-permutation(int_mod)
summary(mod.perm)
par(mfrow=c(1,3))
permutationplot(mod.perm,factor = "sex")
permutationplot(mod.perm,factor = "group")
permutationplot(mod.perm,factor = "sex:group")
```

#### Give explanation of 6F2 here

In ASCA, a permutation test is used instead of a F-test because the
response variable is multivariate and high-dimensional, with many
correlated variables. Metabolomics data typically violate the
assumptions required for an F-test, such as multivariate normality,
independence between variables, and error structure. In addition, the
number of variables often exceeds the number of samples, making
classical parametric tests unreliable.

Permutation testing does not rely on these assumptions. Instead, it
assesses significance by repeatedly permuting the sample labels and
recalculating the explained variance under the null hypothesis of no
association between the experimental factors and the data. This
generates an empirical null distribution, against which the observed
explained variance can be compared. As a result, permutation tests
provide a robust and appropriate way to assess statistical significance
in ASCA.

#### Give interpretation of 6F3 permutation test here

The permutation test shows that all three factors are statistically
significant, with a p-value of 0.00. This indicates that the observed
explained variance for Sex, Group and their interaction term Sex:Group
are much larger than expected under the null hypothesis. This confirms
that these factors have has a significant effect on the metabolomic
profiles.

Overall, the permutation test supports the conclusion that sex is the
main driver of systematic variation in the metabolomics data, with
significant, albeit smaller, effects of training and its interaction
with sex.

#### 

#### 6G)

When exploring the model with interaction, it is common to combine the
interaction effect with one of the main factor effects to improve
interpretation.

1\. Make an ASCA model in which the Sex:Group interaction effect is
combined with the main Group effect.[1]

2.  Make two score plot (using the timeplot function) of the combination
    effect. In the first the PC1 scores of the Females and Males are
    plotted as a function of the Groups. In the second the PC1 scores of
    the Groups are plotted as a function of the Females and Males.[1]

3\. Make a loading plot for PC1 of the combination effect [1].

4\. What is your interpretation of the combination effect (Group +
Sex:Group) [1]

```{r Assignment 6G ASCA Combination model, include=TRUE, eval=TRUE, echo=TRUE, message=FALSE}
# 1

comb_mod<-asca(metab~sex+comb(group+sex:group),long_met_data)
summary(comb_mod)

levs <- levels(long_met_data$sex)
cols <- seq_along(levs)


timeplot(comb_mod,factor = "sex", x_time = T, 
         time="group",
         comb = 2,
         comp = 1,
         xaxt='n',
         main="PC1 score plot as a function of training group per sex",
         ylab="PC1 score",
         xlab = "Sex")

legend("bottomright",
       legend = levs,
       col = cols,
       lwd=2,
       cex = 0.6)

levs <- levels(long_met_data$group)
cols <- seq_along(levs)


timeplot(comb_mod,time='sex',factor="group",comb=2,comp=1,x_time = T,
          main="PC1 score plot as a function of sex per treatment group",
         ylab="PC1 score",
         xlab="Training group")


legend("bottomright",
       legend = levs,
       col = cols,
       lwd=2,
       cex = 0.6)


##
loadingplot(comb_mod,pc=1,factor = "sex",
            main="PC1 loading plot of lipid metabolites",
            xlab="Metabolite",
            ylab="PC1 loading")
        
```

#### Give your interpretation of 6G4 here

The combined effect of Group and Sex:Group explains a substantial
proportion of the structured variation in the metabolomic data (20.51%),
indicating that the training effect cannot be fully interpreted without
considering sex differences.

The timeplots of the PC1 scores show that females and males follow
different response patterns across the training groups, confirming the
presence of a sex-dependent training effect. We observe male subjects
scoring higher along the PC1 axis in the control group and at 8 weeks of
training, while female subjects score higher in the other groups. This
could also suggest an aging related phenomenon, given the similarity
between subjects after 8 weeks.

Across groups, the general trend along PC1 suggests that training
induces systematic changes in the metabolomic profiles, but the
magnitude and direction of these changes differ between sexes. While
both females and males show consistent trends across training duration,
the separation between groups is more pronounced for some time points,
indicating that sex modulates the metabolic response to training rather
than simply shifting all profiles in the same way.

The second timeplot, where PC1 scores are shown as a function of sex for
each group, further supports this interpretation by showing that group
differences are not constant across sexes. This confirms that the
interaction between sex and group contributes meaningfully to the
observed variation.

The loading plot of PC1 shows that the combined effect is driven by a
subset of metabolites with higher loadings, while most metabolites
contribute only weakly. This indicates that the sex-dependent training
response is associated with coordinated changes in a limited number of
metabolites rather than a global shift of the metabolome.

Overall, the combined ASCA model reveals that training induces moderate
but structured metabolic changes that depend on sex, and that combining
the main Group effect with the Sex:Group interaction provides a clearer
and more biologically meaningful interpretation of the training
response.

#### This concludes assignment 6

Make sure you save your .Rmd file and workspace so you can continue in
your next project session.

#### That concludes week 2 of the project!

Please hand in your .rmd and .pdf files on Canvas for your project
grade. The week 2 deadline is Tuesday January 20, 08:00 (AM).\*\*\*
